title: Los Angeles' Homelessness Strategy Needs a Next Act
gist: Drawing on RAND's LA LEADS bimonthly street census of Hollywood, Skid Row, and Venice since 2021, Louis Abramson argues LA's encampment-focused Inside Safe strategy nearly eliminated tent clusters but left a smaller, harder-to-reach unsheltered population dominated by rough sleepers and vehicle dwellers, and calls for a pivot to centralized service hubs to sustain progress.

insights.claim: LA's Inside Safe program, which has targeted tent encampments for relocation to interim housing since 2022, cut the number of tents in Hollywood from roughly 250 to about 30 (a drop of almost 90%), with Venice seeing similar declines.
insights.speaker: Louis Abramson
insights.so_what: Validates encampment-targeted outreach as a highly effective tactic against the easiest-to-reach subset of homelessness, but signals that tactic's addressable population is now largely exhausted outside Skid Row.
insights.quote: the number of tents in Hollywood has fallen from roughly 250 to only about 30—a drop of almost 90%

insights.claim: Over four years of RAND's Rand LA street census, the number of tents in Hollywood, Skid Row, and Venice fell by half while vehicle dwelling rose 20% and rough sleeping increased, leaving the overall unsheltered population 14% smaller but needier, more mobile, and harder to engage.
insights.speaker: Louis Abramson
insights.so_what: The easy-to-reach encampment population has been substantially cleared, so future homelessness-reduction gains will come more slowly and require different, costlier interventions.
insights.quote: Today's unsheltered population is now 14% smaller overall, but needier, more mobile and harder to engage than before.

insights.claim: Rough sleepers are now a plurality of LA's unsheltered population (roughly 40% to 45%) and scored worse than tent dwellers on 14 of 20 well-being indicators, including physical health, mental health, substance use, and experiences with the justice system.
insights.speaker: Louis Abramson
insights.so_what: Identifies the highest-acuity, costliest-to-serve subgroup now driving remaining homelessness counts, relevant to forecasting LA's social-services and behavioral-health spending needs.
insights.quote: rough sleepers scored worse than tent dwellers in 14 of 20 indicators, including physical health, mental health, substance use and experiences with the justice system
insights.locator: Rough sleepers have the highest needs

insights.claim: Rough sleepers' lack of a fixed location, cellphones, or documentation makes the repeat-visit outreach model that worked on tent encampments structurally unworkable for this group.
insights.speaker: Louis Abramson
insights.so_what: Explains why marginal cost per person housed will keep rising under the current outreach model — a structural argument for redesigning the service-delivery model rather than scaling the existing one.
insights.quote: Most also lack cellphones or the documents needed to be placed in housing.
insights.locator: Setting aside their needs, rough sleepers simply have no fixed location

insights.claim: Vehicle dwellers retain an asset (their vehicle) that housing facilities' parking limits may force them to give up; nearly 20% have jobs and 54% report looking for work, making a vehicle more valuable to their path out of homelessness than a bed in a congregate shelter, which they may also view as a safety risk.
insights.speaker: Louis Abramson
insights.so_what: Standard interim-shelter placements are poorly matched to a working, asset-holding subgroup — implies programs need employment-compatible, vehicle-preserving options (e.g., safe parking) rather than one-size-fits-all shelter beds.
insights.quote: nearly 20% of vehicle dwellers have jobs, and 54% report looking for work
insights.locator: Vehicle dwellers, while also mobile, present different challenges

themes.theme: Policy prescription: from outreach to hubs

insights.claim: Abramson argues LA should pivot from an outreach model (bringing help to encampments) to a hub-and-spoke model where outreach workers transport unsheltered people to centralized service hubs, since dispersed populations have made door-to-door outreach far less efficient.
insights.speaker: Louis Abramson
insights.so_what: A concrete, actionable policy redesign proposal from a RAND researcher with direct census data behind it — a candidate model for other cities facing the same encampment-to-dispersed-homelessness transition.
insights.quote: Instead of bringing help to people, we must bring people to centralized service hubs.

insights.claim: Effective service hubs need to offer same-day housing matching, low-barrier behavioral health treatment, on-site documentation clinics, and access to basic cellphones; for vehicle dwellers specifically, the toolkit should add scaled safe-parking programs and employment supports paired with rapid rehousing.
insights.speaker: Louis Abramson
insights.so_what: Specifies the operational feature set a homelessness-services provider or municipal contractor would need to build to serve this population — a checklist for evaluating city RFPs or nonprofit program design.
insights.quote: same-day housing matching, low-barrier behavioral health treatment, on-site documentation clinics and access to basic cellphones

insights.claim: Abramson states that the strategies which succeeded in clearing tent encampments will not get the city the rest of the way toward reducing homelessness, implying LA's recent double-digit percentage declines in unsheltered counts are likely to stall without a hub-based policy shift.
insights.speaker: Louis Abramson
insights.so_what: A caution against extrapolating LA's recent homelessness-reduction trend forward without a strategy change — relevant to evaluating the credibility of city homelessness-spending outcomes.
insights.quote: Strategies that succeeded in reducing that form of homelessness will not get us the rest of the way.

insights.claim: RAND's Rand LA project has conducted a bimonthly census of unsheltered people in Hollywood, Skid Row, and Venice since September 2021 using professional field staff rather than the volunteers used in the annual county-wide homelessness count, tracking not just headcounts but living conditions (tents, vehicles, or rough sleeping).
insights.speaker: Louis Abramson
insights.so_what: Establishes the data source's methodological credibility (professional staff, high frequency) versus the standard annual county count, which is useful context for weighing this data against official city/county homelessness statistics.
insights.quote: Using professional field staff—not volunteers, like the annual county-wide homelessness count—Rand LA has been taking a census of unsheltered people
